name: Custom Built-In-Style Painted Wall Unit with End Towers, Cubbies, and Overhead Cabinets
description: A large, symmetrical, floor-standing custom wall unit in light beige painted wood, composed as one continuous built-in-style piece. Narrow outer framing stiles and a slim full-width top cap enclose two full-height end towers and a broader central section. Each end tower is organized vertically as an upper closed door, a deep open middle niche whose bottom aligns with the main countertop level, and a tall lower closed door. Between the towers, the lower central base forms a deep credenza with a left single-door cupboard, a central bank of six drawers arranged in two columns by three rows, and a right single-door cupboard, all beneath a continuous countertop. Resting on that countertop is a shallower upper-center assembly containing irregular open cubby shelving on both sides of a tall central double-door cabinet with long vertical pulls. Above this upper-center zone, a continuous band of four overhead cabinet doors spans between the towers. The entire piece stands on a continuous low plinth and should read as a custom architectural storage-and-display focal wall unit.

materials.name: painted wood / MDF
materials.usage: primary carcass, doors, drawer fronts, cubby dividers, top cap, countertop, plinth, and outer framing members
materials.finish: light beige painted finish, matte or eggshell

materials.name: painted back panels
materials.usage: backs of open cubbies, tower niches, and cabinet interiors as needed for fabrication
materials.finish: painted to match the main casework

materials.name: metal hardware
materials.usage: surface-mounted pulls on drawers and cabinet doors

colors.notes: high confidence from the written brief; the sketch itself is monochrome

structural_elements.name: continuous top cap
structural_elements.description: A slim full-width top member visually unifying the entire cabinet.

structural_elements.name: outer boundary stiles
structural_elements.description: Narrow vertical framing members defining the far-left and far-right edges of the unit.
structural_elements.position: extreme left and extreme right outer edges

structural_elements.name: continuous base plinth
structural_elements.description: A low continuous base lifting the doors and drawers off the floor line.

structural_elements.name: end storage towers
structural_elements.description: Two full-height deep side towers, each divided vertically into an upper closed cabinet door, a deep open middle niche, and a lower closed cabinet door.

structural_elements.name: lower side base cupboards
structural_elements.description: Single-door base cupboards flanking the central drawer bank.

structural_elements.name: central six-drawer bank
structural_elements.description: A central drawer block with six drawers arranged in two columns by three rows.

structural_elements.name: continuous countertop
structural_elements.description: A continuous horizontal surface spanning between the towers and aligning with the bottoms of the tower middle niches.

structural_elements.name: upper center double-door cabinet
structural_elements.description: A tall central cabinet above the countertop with two plain doors and long vertical bar pulls near the center meeting line.

structural_elements.name: upper irregular cubby display bays
structural_elements.description: Open display/storage bays with staggered, non-uniform dividers creating irregular cubby openings.

structural_elements.name: top row overhead cabinets
structural_elements.description: A continuous upper band divided into four operable cabinet doors spanning the central section between the towers.
structural_elements.position: uppermost central span beneath the top cap

spatial_layout: The composition reads symmetrically as left tower, broad central section, and right tower. In depth, the side towers and lower base credenza occupy the deeper front plane, while the upper-center assembly sits shallower and set back on the countertop. In elevation, each tower stacks upper door, open middle niche, and lower door; the central base stacks a left cupboard, a six-drawer bank, and a right cupboard beneath the countertop; above the countertop sit irregular open cubbies flanking a tall central double-door cabinet, all topped by a four-door overhead band.

context.placement: against a wall as a large floor-standing built-in-style unit
context.surroundings: blank wall or minimal setting; no side wall returns or surrounding room architecture required
context.scale_reference: no reliable scale reference or dimensions are provided

views_to_generate.label: Floor Plan
views_to_generate.description: flat 2D top-down view — camera pointing straight down at the object, showing the full cabinet footprint, the full-depth towers and lower base, and the shallower set-back upper-center footprint
views_to_generate.generation_prompt:
STEP 1 — OVERALL FORM: A flat top-down section diagram of one very wide cabinet, much wider than deep, with a full rectangular deepest footprint and a second shallower rectangle set back inside the rear center.
STEP 2 — OUTER BOUNDARY: Draw a clean thick-line outer rectangle for the full deepest footprint of the entire wall unit, spanning from the far-left outer stile to the far-right outer stile and from the front face of the deep base to the back line of the towers.
STEP 3 — LEFT OUTER FRAME STRIP: At the far-left edge, indicate a very narrow vertical strip just inside the perimeter for the left outer framing stile or side panel.
STEP 4 — LEFT TOWER FOOTPRINT: Immediately inside that strip, draw a full-depth narrow rectangle for the left end tower, equal in depth to the deepest overall footprint and clearly narrower than the broad central span.
STEP 5 — LEFT TOWER ANNOTATION: Lightly label the left tower as a vertically stacked bay containing an upper door, a middle open niche, and a lower door in elevation; do not divide this tower front-to-back because that organization is vertical, not visible in plan.
STEP 6 — CENTRAL DEEP BASE FOOTPRINT: Between the two end towers, draw the full-depth central base zone along the front portion of the cabinet, continuous with the towers at the same front line.
STEP 7 — LEFT BASE CUPBOARD FOOTPRINT: At the left side of that central base zone, mark a narrower rectangular compartment for the left single-door base cupboard and label its front edge DOOR with no swing arc.
STEP 8 — CENTRAL DRAWER BANK FOOTPRINT: In the middle of the deep base, draw the widest central rectangle for the drawer bank; label the front edge DRAWERS and keep this zone wider than either side cupboard.
STEP 9 — RIGHT BASE CUPBOARD FOOTPRINT: At the right side of the deep base zone, add a matching narrower rectangular compartment for the right single-door base cupboard and label its front edge DOOR with no swing arc.
STEP 10 — COUNTERTOP SET-BACK LINE: Behind the deep base compartments, draw a long straight line across the central span marking the front face of the shallower upper-center assembly and the exposed counter ledge in front of it.
STEP 11 — SHALLOW UPPER-CENTER FOOTPRINT: In the rear center between the towers, draw a second shallower rectangle aligned to the back of the unit and set back from the deep base front, clearly showing that the upper-center assembly is shallower than the towers and lower base.
STEP 12 — LEFT UPPER CUBBY BAY FOOTPRINT: Subdivide the left side of that shallow rectangle into a moderate-width bay for the open cubby display area; keep it open-front in concept and label it LEFT CUBBIES.
STEP 13 — CENTER TALL-DOOR CABINET FOOTPRINT: In the middle of the shallow rectangle, draw a central bay for the tall double-door cabinet; label its front edge DOOR and keep this bay similar to or slightly wider than each cubby bay.
STEP 14 — RIGHT UPPER CUBBY BAY FOOTPRINT: Subdivide the right side of the shallow rectangle into a matching bay for the open cubby display area and label it RIGHT CUBBIES.
STEP 15 — OVERHEAD BAND NOTE: Within the same shallow upper-center footprint, note that a continuous top row of four overhead cabinet doors spans across this central upper zone in elevation; do not create a separate deeper plan block for that overhead row.
STEP 16 — RIGHT TOWER FOOTPRINT: At the far right, mirror the left tower with another full-depth narrow rectangle for the right end tower.
STEP 17 — RIGHT TOWER ANNOTATION: Lightly label the right tower as a vertically stacked bay containing an upper door, a middle open niche, and a lower door in elevation; again, do not divide it front-to-back.
STEP 18 — RIGHT OUTER FRAME STRIP: At the extreme right edge, indicate the very narrow right outer framing stile or side panel inside the perimeter.
STEP 19 — LINEWORK AND OPEN-CLOSED LOGIC: Keep all lines crisp and orthographic, with no perspective, no swing arcs, and no curved openings; the only openly accessible front zones in the overall design are the tower middle niches and the upper cubby bays, while the labeled door and drawer zones remain closed fronts.
CONCLUSION: The completed drawing should read as a flat labeled top-down cabinet diagram showing one full-depth rectangular wall unit with deep end towers and deep lower base wrapped around a shallower set-back upper-center storage and display assembly.

views_to_generate.label: Front Elevation — Main Cabinet Face
views_to_generate.description: frontal 2D view of the cabinet's main face, showing the full outer frame, plinth, doors, drawers, tower niches, cubbies, overhead row, countertop, and hardware
views_to_generate.generation_prompt:
STEP 1 — OVERALL FORM: Draw a flat front elevation of one wide landscape cabinet, substantially wider than tall, composed as a symmetrical wall unit with narrower end towers and a broader central section.
STEP 2 — OUTER BOUNDARY: Enclose everything within one clean outer rectangle, then add a slim full-width top cap at the very top and a low continuous plinth band at the very bottom.
STEP 3 — LEFT OUTER STILE: At the far-left edge, draw a very narrow full-height vertical framing strip that reads as the left outer stile, not as a separate cabinet bay.
STEP 4 — LEFT TOWER OVERALL: Immediately to the right of that strip, draw the left end tower as a narrow full-height vertical bay extending from the plinth to just under the top cap.
STEP 5 — LEFT TOWER UPPER DOOR: In the upper part of the left tower, draw one plain closed door panel filling the tower width, with a small simple surface pull placed near its inner opening edge.
STEP 6 — LEFT TOWER MIDDLE NICHE: Below that upper door, draw one open rectangular niche with no door, no handle, and no extra face lines; keep its bottom aligned with the main countertop level that crosses the central section.
STEP 7 — LEFT TOWER LOWER DOOR: Below the open niche, draw one tall plain lower door panel filling the tower width down toward the plinth, again with a small simple surface pull near the inner opening edge.
STEP 8 — CENTRAL LOWER SECTION OVERALL: Between the two towers, draw a broad lower base zone from the plinth up to the continuous countertop line; this lower zone contains one cupboard, one wide six-drawer block, and one cupboard.
STEP 9 — LEFT BASE CUPBOARD: At the left side of the lower central zone, draw a single plain cupboard door, narrower than the drawer bank, with one small simple pull near its inner opening edge.
STEP 10 — CENTRAL DRAWER BANK FRAME: In the exact center of the lower base, draw the largest lower block as a six-drawer arrangement in two equal vertical columns and three horizontal rows.
STEP 11 — DRAWER BANK TOP ROW: Draw two side-by-side top drawers of equal width, each with one centered small pull.
STEP 12 — DRAWER BANK MIDDLE ROW: Directly below, draw two matching middle drawers, again equal in width and each with one centered small pull.
STEP 13 — DRAWER BANK LOWER ROW: Directly below, draw two matching bottom drawers, again equal in width and each with one centered small pull.
STEP 14 — RIGHT BASE CUPBOARD: At the right side of the lower central zone, draw a cupboard door matching the left base cupboard, with one small simple pull near its inner opening edge.
STEP 15 — CONTINUOUS COUNTERTOP: Draw one strong straight horizontal countertop line across the full central span between towers, with the bottoms of both tower middle niches aligning to this same level.
STEP 16 — UPPER CENTRAL SECTION OVERALL: Above the countertop and between the towers, draw a shallower-looking upper composition in elevation, organized as a top overhead band and, below that, left cubbies, center double doors, and right cubbies.
STEP 17 — TOP OVERHEAD BAND: Directly beneath the top cap and spanning only between the towers, draw a continuous horizontal band divided into four near-equal cabinet doors; place one small simple surface pull on each door and keep this band separate from the tower upper doors.
STEP 18 — LEFT UPPER CUBBY OUTLINE: Below the overhead band and left of the central tall doors, draw one open rectangular bay for the left cubby field, extending down to the countertop.
STEP 19 — LEFT UPPER CUBBY INTERNAL PARTITIONS: Inside that left cubby bay, create a staggered irregular grid using several short shelf lines and several vertical dividers so that no shelf line runs cleanly all the way across; include a mix of smaller and larger openings, keep the geometry visually balanced, and add no hardware.
STEP 20 — CENTRAL TALL DOUBLE DOORS: In the exact upper center, draw one tall cabinet formed by two plain vertical doors meeting at the center seam, extending from the countertop up to just below the overhead band; place one long vertical bar pull on each door close to the center meeting line.
STEP 21 — RIGHT UPPER CUBBY OUTLINE: To the right of the central tall doors, draw a matching open bay for the right cubby field, extending from the countertop up to just below the overhead band.
STEP 22 — RIGHT UPPER CUBBY INTERNAL PARTITIONS: Inside that right cubby bay, create another irregular staggered grid of shelves and dividers comparable in density to the left side but not a rigid repetitive shelf system; keep it open, asymmetrical, and free of hardware.
STEP 23 — RIGHT TOWER OVERALL: At the far right beyond the central section, draw the right end tower matching the left tower in width and full height.
STEP 24 — RIGHT TOWER UPPER DOOR: In the upper part of the right tower, draw one plain closed door panel with a small simple surface pull near its inner opening edge.
STEP 25 — RIGHT TOWER MIDDLE NICHE: Below that upper door, draw one open rectangular middle niche with no hardware, matching the left tower niche in height and alignment.
STEP 26 — RIGHT TOWER LOWER DOOR: Below the niche, draw one tall plain lower door panel with a small simple surface pull near its inner opening edge.
STEP 27 — RIGHT OUTER STILE: At the extreme right edge, finish with a very narrow full-height vertical framing strip matching the left outer stile.
STEP 28 — BASE AND GROUND LINE: Under the plinth, add a thin continuous ground line across the full width of the drawing; keep the plinth continuous under towers and central base alike.
STEP 29 — HARDWARE AND OPEN-CLOSED CHECK: Ensure that hardware appears only on the four overhead doors, the two tall central doors, the two base cupboards, the six drawers, and the upper and lower tower doors; the cubbies and tower middle niches must remain completely open and undecorated.
CONCLUSION: The completed elevation should read as one large symmetrical built-in-style cabinet with deep end towers, a drawer-and-cupboard base, a central counter, irregular open cubbies, tall central double doors, and a four-door overhead band, all in a clean flat front view.

generation_notes: Produce only flat orthographic drawings, never perspective or three-dimensional views. Use light beige painted wood throughout. Encode open versus closed zones strictly: the only open front zones are the middle niches of both end towers and the irregular upper cubby bays; all other marked fronts are closed and operable. Show small simple surface pulls on the tower doors, lower side cupboards, six drawers, and four overhead cabinet doors, and show long vertical bar pulls only on the tall central double doors. Apply the council majority for the continuous band of four overhead cabinet doors, the continuous low base plinth, the stepped depth profile with deeper towers and base and a shallower upper-center assembly, and narrow outer framing stiles. Do not literally reproduce the sketch's X symbols; translate them into plain narrow outer framing members. Treat the piece as built-in-style but self-contained against a blank wall. Keep all dimensions null and unstated. Preserve the cubby areas as complex irregular staggered partitions; if exact micro-geometry is uncertain, favor visually balanced asymmetry rather than evenly spaced shelves or rigidly repetitive grids.
